learn.title: How Wind Turbines Generate Electricity
learn.intro: Wind energy is a cornerstone of the global transition to net-zero. By capturing the kinetic energy of moving air, turbines provide a scalable, carbon-free alternative to fossil fuels. Understanding the physics behind these giants reveals how nature's breeze is converted into the electricity that powers our homes and industries without emitting greenhouse gases.
learn.c1_text: Turbine blades act like airplane wings. As wind flows over the curved blade, it creates a pressure difference that generates 'lift,' pushing the blade forward. Think of it like sticking your hand out a car window; the air pushes your hand up, turning that linear wind force into rotational motion.
learn.c2_text: The rotating blades turn a shaft connected to a gearbox, which speeds up the rotation for the generator. Inside, magnets spin rapidly around copper wire coils. This process, called electromagnetic induction, converts the mechanical energy of the spinning shaft into an electrical current that can be sent to the grid.
learn.c3_text: India leverages its vast coastline and plains, particularly in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat. The government's focus on 'repowering' older turbines with newer, high-efficiency models ensures that existing wind farms produce significantly more power from the same land area, aligning with the National Wind-Solar Hybrid Policy.
learn.stat3: Blade tip speeds: Up to 200 mph
learn.readLabel: Read at US Dept of Energy

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels: Power from Both Sides
learn.intro: Standard solar panels only capture light from the front, wasting the energy that hits the back. Bifacial technology changes the game by capturing reflected sunlight, significantly increasing energy yield per square meter. This efficiency boost is critical for reducing the land footprint of massive solar parks and lowering the overall cost of solar energy.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels use a transparent backsheet to capture 'albedo'—the light reflected from the ground. It is like using a mirror to bounce light into a dark room; the brighter the surface below (like white gravel or snow), the more extra electricity the panel generates.
learn.c2_text: Unlike monofacial cells, bifacial cells are engineered to be active on both sides. This allows them to harvest direct sunlight on top and diffuse, reflected light on the bottom. This can increase total energy production by 10% to 30% depending on the surface reflectivity.
learn.c3_text: India is increasingly adopting bifacial modules in utility-scale projects like those in Rajasthan. Because the sandy soil provides a decent albedo effect, developers are seeing higher kWh yields, helping India move toward its ambitious 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity target by 2030.

learn.title: EV Charging Infrastructure Levels Explained
learn.intro: The transition to electric vehicles depends entirely on a reliable charging network. Without accessible 'fueling' options, range anxiety prevents adoption. Understanding the difference between charging levels helps users choose the right equipment and helps planners design grids that can handle the increased electrical load without crashing.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses a standard 120V household outlet. It is like filling a swimming pool with a garden hose; it's very slow, adding about 3-5 miles of range per hour. It is best for plug-in hybrids or drivers with very short daily commutes.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 uses 240V (like a dryer outlet) for overnight charging. DC Fast Charging bypasses the car's onboard converter to pump high-voltage power directly into the battery. This is like using a fire hose, charging a battery to 80% in 20-40 minutes.
learn.c3_text: India is expanding its infrastructure via the FAME-II scheme, focusing on public charging hubs. The government is encouraging the deployment of DC fast chargers along national highways to enable long-distance EV travel between major cities like Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru.
learn.stat3: DC Fast Charging: 50 kW to 350 kW
learn.readLabel: Read at US Dept of Energy

learn.title: Green Ammonia: The Future of Clean Fuel
learn.intro: Ammonia is already produced globally for fertilizer, but the process is carbon-heavy. 'Green' ammonia replaces fossil fuels with renewable electricity, turning a pollutant-heavy industry into a carbon-free one. It is a vital 'energy carrier' because it is easier to store and transport than pure hydrogen.
learn.c1_text: Green ammonia is made by combining nitrogen from the air with hydrogen produced via water electrolysis using wind or solar power. It is like a chemical battery; you store renewable energy in a liquid form that can be shipped across oceans in tankers.
learn.c2_text: Ammonia is an ideal shipping fuel because it has a higher energy density than liquid hydrogen. It can also be used directly as a carbon-free fertilizer, removing the CO2 emissions usually associated with the Haber-Bosch process used in traditional ammonia plants.
learn.c3_text: India, one of the world's largest fertilizer consumers, is pivoting toward green ammonia to reduce import dependency. Major firms like Reliance and Adani are investing in gigafactories to produce green ammonia for both domestic agriculture and export to Europe.
learn.stat3: CO2 reduction: ~2 tonnes per tonne of green NH3

learn.title: Understanding the Hydrogen Colour Code
learn.intro: Hydrogen is a versatile fuel, but not all hydrogen is created equal. The 'color code' is a shorthand used by policymakers and engineers to describe the carbon footprint of the production process. To achieve true decarbonization, the world must shift from 'dirty' colors to 'green'.
learn.c1_text: Grey hydrogen comes from natural gas (methane) and releases CO2. Blue hydrogen uses the same process but adds Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) to trap the emissions. Think of Blue as Grey hydrogen wearing a 'filter' to keep the air clean.
learn.c2_text: Green hydrogen is produced via electrolysis, using renewable electricity to split water (H2O) into hydrogen and oxygen. There are zero carbon emissions involved. It is the 'gold standard' for a sustainable energy economy, though currently more expensive to produce.
learn.c3_text: India has launched a massive mission to become a global hub for green hydrogen. By utilizing its cheap solar power, India aims to produce 5 million metric tonnes of green hydrogen per year by 2030, targeting hard-to-abate sectors like steel and refineries.
learn.stat3: India's goal: 5 MMT green H2 by 2030

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Beyond Silicon
learn.intro: Silicon has dominated solar for decades, but it is reaching its theoretical efficiency limit. Perovskites—a class of materials with a specific crystal structure—promise higher efficiency, lower production costs, and flexibility. They could turn every window and wall into a power generator.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites are synthetic materials that mimic a specific mineral structure. They are like 'tunable' sponges for light; scientists can tweak their chemistry to absorb different parts of the solar spectrum more effectively than rigid silicon crystals can.
learn.c2_text: The real power comes from 'Tandem' cells, where a layer of perovskite is placed on top of silicon. The perovskite captures high-energy blue light, while the silicon captures lower-energy red light. This 'tag-team' approach pushes efficiency past the 30% mark.
learn.c3_text: Indian institutes like IITs are researching perovskites to create flexible, lightweight solar films. These could be integrated into urban architecture in crowded cities like Mumbai, where roof space is limited but vertical wall space is abundant.
